title: How a Wife Can File for Divorce in Bangladesh (2026) – Khul, Court & Legal Options
metaTitle: Wife Filing for Divorce in Bangladesh (2026) – Khul, Family Court & All Legal Options
metaDescription: Wife wanting a divorce in Bangladesh? Full 2026 guide — khul divorce, Family Court petition, mahr rights, child custody & step-by-step process. Expert lawyer guide.
keywords: how wife can get divorce in bangladesh
divorce procedure in bangladesh by wife
heroIntro: A wife in Bangladesh has multiple legal rights and pathways to divorce — and no court can force her to remain in a marriage against her will. This 2026 guide explains every option available to a wife seeking divorce: khul (mutual or court-ordered), Family Court petition, and what financial rights she retains throughout the process.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: How a Wife Gets Divorced in Bangladesh
points: Option 1 – Khul (husband agrees): File notice at Union Council → 90-day period → divorce final
Option 2 – Judicial Khul (husband refuses): File petition in Family Court → court orders divorce
Option 3 – Delegated Talaq: If Kabinnama grants wife right to talaq — she can pronounce it directly
Option 4 – Judicial Divorce (Faskh): File in Family Court on grounds like cruelty or desertion
Financial rights always include: mahr (dower), iddat maintenance, child maintenance
Husband cannot prevent wife from getting a divorce — courts always have jurisdiction

sections.h2: Can a Wife File for Divorce in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Yes — absolutely. A Muslim wife in Bangladesh has the legal right to dissolve her marriage through multiple avenues. This right is protected under:

  • Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 (MFLO) — establishes the arbitration and notice process
  • Family Courts Ordinance 1985 — gives Family Court exclusive jurisdiction, including wife's divorce petitions
  • Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939 — specifies statutory grounds on which a wife can seek judicial divorce

A husband cannot legally prevent his wife from obtaining a divorce. The Family Court has the power to grant a divorce decree even against the husband's wishes. No wife is forced to remain in a marriage simply because her husband refuses to agree. For immediate legal assistance, contact Advocate Md. Shah Alam's family law chamber in Uttara, Dhaka.

Khul is the wife's right to dissolve the marriage by returning a consideration (typically the mahr/dower) to the husband in exchange for agreeing to end the marriage. When the husband agrees, this is the fastest route:

  1. Wife and husband agree on the amount of compensation (usually the mahr amount)
  2. Execute a khulnama (written khul agreement)
  3. Submit notice to the local Union Parishad/City Corporation Chairman
  4. Mandatory 90-day reconciliation and iddat period
  5. After 90 days, divorce is final and a certificate is issued

Timeline: Minimum 90 days from date of notice
Cost: BDT 5,000–15,000 total including lawyer and registration fees
Key Point: The wife does not need to return the entire mahr — the parties can negotiate the consideration amount

sections.h2: Option 2: Judicial Khul Through Family Court
sections.content:
If the husband refuses to agree to khul, the wife can file a petition in the Family Court. The court has the absolute power to grant a khul even without the husband's consent:

  1. File petition in Family Court: The wife (plaintiff) files a divorce petition through her lawyer, citing the khul claim
  2. Notice served to husband: The court serves notice to the husband to appear
  3. Reconciliation attempt by court: The court attempts reconciliation — the husband can argue his case, but cannot prevent the divorce
  4. Court hearing and evidence: Multiple hearings — wife presents her evidence, husband responds
  5. Divorce decree: Court issues final divorce decree and determines appropriate compensation

⚠️ Critical point: Even if the husband refuses, does not appear, or contests — the court will still grant the divorce. Delay by the husband is a delay tactic, not a permanent obstruction. A skilled family lawyer in Dhaka can move the case forward efficiently.

Timeline: 6–18 months (can be faster if uncontested after filing)
Cost: BDT 20,000–50,000 including lawyer fees

If the wife's Kabinnama (marriage contract) includes a clause delegating the right of talaq to the wife, she can pronounce talaq directly — without the husband's consent.

This is called Talaq-e-Tafwid (delegated divorce). The process is:

  1. Check if the Kabinnama contains a delegation clause (Column 18 or 19 in standard Kabinnama forms)
  2. Wife pronounces talaq under the delegated power
  3. Follow the same MFLO 1961 notice procedure — submit written notice to Union Council Chairman within 30 days
  4. 90-day iddat/reconciliation period
  5. Divorce becomes final

⚠️ Many Kabinanamas do not contain this delegation. Check your Kabinnama with a lawyer to confirm. If planning to marry in future, consider insisting on this clause being included in your Kabinnama — it is a significant legal protection for the wife.

sections.h2: Option 4: Judicial Divorce (Faskh) on Statutory Grounds
sections.content:
Under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939, a wife may seek judicial divorce from the Family Court on any of the following grounds:

  • Cruelty (physical or mental): Domestic violence, torture, threats, severe emotional abuse
  • Desertion: Husband has deserted the wife for two or more years
  • Failure to maintain: Husband has not provided maintenance for two or more years
  • Husband's imprisonment: Serving a sentence of 7 or more years
  • Husband's impotency: Existing at the time of marriage
  • Husband's insanity: Continuous for two or more years
  • Communicable disease (e.g., leprosy, HIV/AIDS)
  • Polygamy without permission: Second marriage taken without Arbitration Council's prior approval

For domestic violence cases — evidence such as medical reports, police GD records, witness testimony, photos and medical records strengthen the case significantly. A divorce lawyer in Uttara can help you build this evidence before filing.

sections.h2: Documents a Wife Needs for Divorce
sections.content:
For any divorce proceedings as a wife, gather the following:

  • Original Kabinnama (marriage certificate) — certified copy if original is with husband
  • Your National Identity Card (NID)
  • Children's birth certificates (if applicable)
  • If applying for khul with mahr waiver: any mahr receipt or payment records
  • For cruelty grounds: medical reports, police GD records, hospital records, photographs, witness names
  • For non-maintenance: bank statements, bills, evidence of financial hardship
  • For desertion: evidence of husband's absence — correspondence records, address proof

If the original Kabinnama is in the husband's possession and he refuses to return it: the court can compel production, or you can obtain a certified copy from the Kazi who registered the marriage or the local Registration Office.

sections.h2: Wife's Financial Rights During and After Divorce
sections.content:
Regardless of which divorce route is taken, a wife is entitled to the following financial rights:

1. Mahr (Dower)

The wife is entitled to her full deferred mahr amount stated in the Kabinnama. In a khul where wife returns the mahr as compensation, this right is waived as part of the agreement — but only to the extent agreed.

2. Iddat Maintenance

The husband must maintain the wife during the 90-day iddat period following divorce — even if he initiated the divorce. This is a legal obligation.

3. Past Maintenance (Nafaqah)

A wife can claim up to 3 years of unpaid maintenance that the husband failed to provide during the marriage — as a separate claim in Family Court.

4. Child Maintenance

The father is always obligated to maintain children financially, regardless of who has physical custody. The Family Court sets the monthly amount based on the father's income and the child's needs.

5. Child Custody (Hizanat)

Under Islamic law, mothers typically retain custody of young children — sons until age 7, daughters until puberty. After these ages, the court weighs the best interests of the child and may award custody to either parent.

sections.h2: What If the Husband Refuses to Agree or Sign?
sections.content:
The husband's refusal to cooperate does NOT prevent a wife from getting divorced in Bangladesh. Here is how to handle common obstruction tactics:

  • Refuses to sign khulnama: → File for judicial khul in Family Court. The court grants divorce without husband's signature.
  • Does not appear in court: → Court issues an ex-parte decree after proper notice. Case proceeds without him.
  • Threatens or pressures wife: → Simultaneously apply for a Protection Order under the Domestic Violence (Prevention and Protection) Act 2010 — provides injunction against harassment.
  • Hides the Kabinnama: → Court orders production of documents. Kazi registration records are available as certified copies.
  • Delays through repeated adjournments: → Your lawyer files applications to prevent unnecessary delays. Courts have rules against abuse of process.

Do not be intimidated by a husband's threats or refusal to cooperate. Contact our family law team in Uttara, Dhaka for confidential advice and immediate legal action.

sections.h2: How Long Does the Process Take?
sections.content:
Divorce Type Minimum Time Estimated Cost
Khul by Mutual Agreement 90 days BDT 5,000–15,000
Judicial Khul (contested) 6–18 months BDT 20,000–50,000
Delegated Talaq (if Kabinnama clause exists) 90 days BDT 5,000–12,000
Judicial Divorce (Faskh) - cruelty/desertion 8–24 months BDT 25,000–70,000

faqs.question: Can a wife get a divorce without the husband's consent in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Through judicial khul in the Family Court, a wife can obtain a divorce even without the husband's consent. The court will grant the divorce decree regardless of whether the husband agrees — his refusal cannot prevent the divorce forever.

faqs.question: Does a wife have to return the mahr to get a divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: For khul divorce, the wife typically returns the mahr as consideration for the husband's cooperation. However, in a judicial khul where the court orders the divorce, the court determines the appropriate compensation — which may be less than the full mahr amount based on circumstances.

faqs.question: What happens to child custody when a wife files for divorce?
faqs.answer: Child custody proceedings can run simultaneously with divorce proceedings. Under Islamic law (hizanat), mothers have preferential custody rights — sons until age 7, daughters until puberty. The court ultimately decides based on the child's best interests. The father is always required to pay child maintenance.

faqs.question: How does a wife get divorce if the Kabinnama has been kept by the husband?
faqs.answer: The court can compel the husband to produce the Kabinnama, or you can obtain a certified copy from the Kazi who registered the marriage or the district registration office. Lack of a Kabinnama copy does not prevent divorce proceedings.

faqs.question: Can a domestic violence victim get a faster divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. In domestic violence cases, a wife can simultaneously file for a Protection Order (Emergency/Temporary) under the Domestic Violence Act 2010 while pursuing divorce. The Protection Order provides immediate legal protection against harassment or further violence while the divorce case proceeds.
